title: Fondant draping
subtitle: Cake decorating technique for covering a cake smoothly with rolled fondant
excerpt: The technique for covering a cake with a smooth, crease-free layer of rolled fondant. The cake is first crumb-coated with a thin layer of apricot glaze or buttercream to give the fondant something to adhere to. The fondant is then kneaded warm, rolled to a large thin sheet, draped over the cake in a single lift, and smoothed down the sides before trimming. The key challenge is eliminating air bubbles and folds.
sourceNotes: Rolling fondant icing and draping it over a cake to achieve a smooth, professional finish is a technique documented in British cake decorating literature from the twentieth century. The approach of warming and kneading ready-to-roll fondant before draping, then smoothing with the palms, is consistent across British sugarcraft and cake decorating manuals. The crumb coat of sieved apricot jam as an adhesive for fondant on sponge cakes is described in British patisserie literature.

content.text: A smooth apricot crumb coat and warm, well-kneaded fondant make everything easier.
must be done in one continuous session before it skins over. Have the crumb-coated cake ready and the fondant rolled before you drape.

attrs.prepNote: for a 20cm round cake 10cm tall; increase proportionally for larger cakes
or cornflour, for rolling the surface
warmed and sieved, as adhesive layer

content.text: Apply the crumb coat: warm the sieved apricot jam in a small saucepan until runny. Brush a thin, even layer over the entire surface of the chilled cake (top and sides) using the icing spatula. The jam fills any crumbs and gives the fondant an adhesive surface. Leave for 10 minutes to become slightly tacky.

Prepare the fondant: knead the rolled fondant on a clean surface for 2 to 3 minutes until it is warm, smooth, and pliable. Dust the work surface lightly with icing sugar or cornflour. Roll the fondant into a circle large enough to cover the top and sides of the cake with about 5cm of overhang all around. For a 20cm cake 10cm tall the fondant sheet needs to be about 40cm across. Roll to about 4mm thick and lift it periodically to prevent sticking, re-dusting the surface as needed.

Drape and smooth: lift the fondant over the rolling pin and position it over the cake. Unroll it so it falls over the cake centred. Smooth the top first, then ease the fondant down the sides, smoothing out folds as you work. Do not stretch the fondant: lift and reposition folds rather than pulling. Trim the excess at the base with a sharp knife, cutting straight down against the cake board or turntable. Run your palm around the cake to close the seam. Leave to set for at least 30 minutes before decorating.
